Job 1 introduces us not to a man broken by suffering, but to a man stripped of everything that convinced him he was blessed. The chapter opens with a portrait of integrity: Job fears God genuinely, avoids evil deliberately, and his life overflows with the visible markers of divine favor. His wealth is staggering. His family is numerous and apparently close. His piety is so scrupulous that he regularly makes burnt offerings on behalf of his children, anxious that they might have sinned in their hearts even during innocent celebrations. Everything about Job suggests a man whose faith is rewarded, whose righteousness is vindicated by prosperity. But the text will not let us rest in this comfortable arrangement.

Then God Himself draws Satan's attention to Job, essentially inviting the accusation that follows. Satan's taunt cuts to the heart of everything that looks virtuous in Job's life: Does Job fear God for nothing? Is not his piety merely the fruit of his prosperity? Strip away the hedge of protection, destroy the possessions, and Job will curse God to His face. This is the question that haunts the entire book: Can a human being love and trust God when loving and trusting God costs everything? Can righteousness persist when it brings no reward? The stakes could not be higher. God grants Satan permission to destroy Job's property and children, but not to harm Job's body. What follows is a cascade of catastrophe delivered with almost unbearable speed—servants slaughtered, animals consumed, and finally, in a single devastating report, all ten of Job's children dead.

Job's response shatters every comfortable theology of reward. He tears his robe, shaves his head, falls to the ground, and worships. His words are neither complaint nor bargaining: "Naked I came from my mother's womb, and naked I will return. The LORD gave, and the LORD has taken away. Blessed be the name of the LORD." Job acknowledges that he owns nothing—that everything he possessed was always God's to give and to take. He does not sin, and he does not charge God with wrongdoing. But notice what the text does not tell us: it does not tell us why Job maintains his faith. It offers no explanation of how he endures this moment. We are left only with the fact of his faithfulness, and with the terrible question hanging over everything: Is Job's faith genuine, or has the test simply not yet gone deep enough?
